suggested_topics: What would happen if breakfast cereal was legally classified as candy?
Is the ‘productivity hack’ culture just a disguised form of self-surveillance?
Name a food that tastes better because of childhood trauma. Explain.
Has smartphone etiquette genuinely made public spaces more or less hostile?
How would sports change if teams were chosen randomly every season?
extra_system_rules: Sound like a real person on Reddit or Twitter, NOT like a philosophy textbook.
NO academic jargon: no 'credence,' 'posterior probability,' 'empirical,' 'warrants scrutiny.'
Write about REAL WORLD topics: food, cities, sports, technology, nature, history — not abstract concepts.

formats.name: reverse_advice
formats.instruction: Share the worst advice you ever received, in one quick sentence. Make it witty or absurd. No explanations allowed.

formats.name: phantom_dialogue
formats.instruction: Write a mini-script as if two inanimate objects are discussing today's topic. Keep it under 50 words, capture their unique 'personalities.'

formats.name: unexpected_pairing
formats.instruction: Describe how two completely unrelated things (objects, ideas, people) could be combined for a creative outcome. Use a single paragraph (60-120 words), surprise the reader.

formats.name: time_capsule_note
formats.instruction: Write a note as if someone in 2030 is reading this post. Briefly explain why today’s topic would puzzle or delight them. Use 70-150 words.

formats.name: sideways_solution
formats.instruction: Propose a totally unconventional fix for a familiar problem in 1-2 sentences. Make it zany or thought-provoking. Don’t justify, just pitch.

formats.name: hypothetical_history
formats.instruction: Invent an alternative timeline where the topic changed history—describe the ripple effects in 200-300 words with vivid details. Don’t reference reality.

formats.name: picture_puzzle
formats.instruction: Describe an imaginary photo related to the topic—use sensory details and paint a surreal image in 30-60 words. Ask readers to guess what’s happening.

formats.name: micromanifesto
formats.instruction: Declare your stance on a tiny issue with a 3-sentence manifesto. Be punchy and unapologetic, but never use 'I think' or 'maybe.'

formats.name: echo_of_memory
formats.instruction: Recall a fleeting moment tied to the topic. Describe it as a sensory flashback, no more than 40 words. Let nostalgia or emotion lead.

formats.name: scientist_vs_artist
formats.instruction: Write two short paragraphs: first, explain the topic like a scientist; second, reinterpret it as an artist. Each side gets 80-150 words. Contrast starkly.

title_styles: Write a title that challenges common assumptions. Example: 'Why your favorite productivity tip might actually slow you down.'
Create a title as a poetic riddle. Example: 'Where the city never sleeps, who listens to its dreams?'
Craft a title that frames the topic as a mini-mystery. Example: 'The strange object found in my bag—can anyone identify it?'
Compose a title with a mischievous promise. Example: 'In five minutes, you'll see socks differently forever.'
Write a title as a 'what if' accident. Example: 'What if WiFi vanished for a week?'
structure_variants: Start with a boundary-pushing question, respond with two contrasting perspectives, close with a visual analogy.
Begin with a rapid-fire list (5 items), expand on your favorite pick, conclude by rating its upside and downside.
Open with a direct quote, contextualize its relevance, and frame a question that forces the reader to reconsider their stance.
Kick off with a vivid event, move to logical reasoning, wrap up in a single sentence that flips the original event's meaning.
Begin with a metaphorical comparison, dissect it using real-life examples, finish with a paradox that unsettles expectations.
topic_angles: How invisible labor shapes daily routines but rarely gets credit.
The use of personal rituals to anchor chaos during unpredictable times.
Why ‘boring’ technology often outlasts trendy innovation.
What happens when accidental discoveries become purposeful tools.
Social consequences of designing for convenience everywhere.
